message.content:
You are helping golf a Python one-liner for an ARC task. Work ONLY in /Users/jackson/Desktop/code-golf/deepseek-v4-pro-default/task004/. Do NOT modify any files except NEW files you create named agentC_alt_*.py in the workdir/ subfolder.

CONTEXT: Verified 86-byte solution:
```
p=lambda g:[[[y,x][u<any(b)]for x,y,u in zip([0]+a,a,[0]+b)]for a,b in zip(g,g[1:]+g)]
```
Rule: output = input shape shifted RIGHT by 1, EXCEPT the bottom row of each shape stays and the right-edge "cap" cell stays. Precise per-cell: out[r][c] = g[r][c-1] if (g[r+1][c-1]==0 and any(g[r+1])) else g[r][c]. Output col 0 always 0. Row g[0] always all-zeros. Each row has a single color.

Ready checker: workdir/agentC_check.py exposes check(code_string) -> (ok, nbytes). Or run:
`python3 workdir/agentC_check.py 'FULL_CODE'` from the task004 dir; prints `bytes N OK N` on full pass (3 validate + 262 arc-gen + 2000 random), else FAIL.

YOUR GOAL: Find a STRICTLY SHORTER (< 86 bytes) working `p=...` using a FUNDAMENTALLY DIFFERENT structure than the per-column zip. Explore:
- whole-grid operations: build a right-shifted grid S=[[0]+r[:-1] for r in g] and combine with g via a mask
- comparing each row to the shifted row-below using zip of whole rows
- using map/reduce/enumerate
- computing via differences or using the below-row shifted
- def p(g): with statements if it helps (but lambda is usually shorter)
- any trick that avoids the double zip

Test EVERY candidate with the checker; only keep ones that print OK. Report ONLY the shortest verified-OK code and exact byte count. If you cannot beat 86, honestly report the best OK code you confirmed. Only report codes you actually verified print OK.

attachment.content:
- web-design
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

"""Generator."""

import common


def generate(width=None, height=None, rows=None, cols=None, wides=None,
             talls=None, colors=None):
  """Returns input and output grids according to the given parameters.

  Args:
    width: the width of the input grid
    height: the height of the input grid
    rows: a list of vertical coordinates where pixels should be placed
    cols: a list of horizontal coordinates where pixels should be placed
    wides: a list of box widths
    talls: a list of box heights
    colors: a list of colors to be used for pixels
  """
  if rows is None:
    width, height = common.randint(8, 16), common.randint(8, 16)
    rows, cols, wides, talls = [], [], [], []
    row = 1
    while True:
      wide = common.randint(4, width - 1)
      tall = common.randint(3, wide - 1)
      if row + tall > height: break
      col = common.randint(0, width - wide)
      rows.append(row)
      cols.append(col)
      wides.append(wide)
      talls.append(tall)
      row += tall + 1
    colors = common.random_colors(len(rows))

  grid, output = common.grids(width, height)
  for row, col, wide, tall, color in zip(rows, cols, wides, talls, colors):
    # Horizontal stuff.
    for c in range(wide - tall + 2):
      grid[row][col + c] = color
      grid[row + tall - 1][col + c + tall - 2] = color
      output[row][col + c + 1] = color
      output[row + tall - 1][col + c + tall - 2] = color
    # Diagonal stuff
    for r in range(1, tall - 1):
      grid[row + r][col + r - 1] = color
      grid[row + r][col + r + wide - tall + 1] = color
      output[row + r][col + r] = color
      output[row + r][min(col + r + wide - tall + 2, col + wide - 1)] = color
  return {"input": grid, "output": output}



def validate():
  """Validates the generator."""
  train = [
      generate(width=9, height=14, rows=[1, 7], cols=[1, 2], wides=[6, 4],
               talls=[5, 3], colors=[6, 2]),
      generate(width=9, height=8, rows=[1], cols=[1], wides=[8], talls=[5],
               colors=[8]),
  ]
  test = [
      generate(width=10, height=10, rows=[1], cols=[1], wides=[9], talls=[5],
               colors=[4]),
  ]
  return {"train": train, "test": test}

import os
import sys
import importlib.util

# Ensure the script's own directory is on sys.path
sys.path.insert(0, os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)))

from gen import validate


def load_p(filepath):
    """Dynamically import module at filepath and return its p function."""
    spec = importlib.util.spec_from_file_location("candidate", filepath)
    mod = importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec)
    spec.loader.exec_module(mod)
    if not hasattr(mod, "p"):
        raise AttributeError(f"{filepath} does not define a 'p' function")
    return mod.p


def test_all(p, data):
    """Test p against every example in data (train + test), in order."""
    failed = 0
    passed = 0

    for split in ("train", "test"):
        for i, example in enumerate(data[split]):
            inp, expected = example["input"], example["output"]
            try:
                got = p(inp)
            except Exception as e:
                print(f"[{split} {i}] ERROR: {e}")
                failed += 1
                continue

            if got == expected:
                print(f"[{split} {i}] PASS")
                passed += 1
            else:
                print(f"[{split} {i}] FAIL")
                print(f"  Input:\n{format_grid(inp)}")
                print(f"  Expected:\n{format_grid(expected)}")
                print(f"  Got:\n{format_grid(got)}")
                failed += 1

    print(f"\n---")
    print(f"Passed: {passed}, Failed: {failed}")
    return failed == 0


def format_grid(grid):
    """Pretty-print a 2D grid. All cells padded to the width of the widest value."""
    flat = [str(c) for row in grid for c in row]
    width = max(len(v) for v in flat) if flat else 1
    lines = []
    for row in grid:
        lines.append("  " + " ".join(str(c).rjust(width) for c in row))
    return "\n".join(lines)


def main():
    if len(sys.argv) > 1:
        filepath = sys.argv[1]
    else:
        filepath = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "baseline.py")

    # Print code length
    with open(filepath, "r") as f:
        chars = len(f.read())
    print(f"Code length: {chars} chars\n")

    try:
        p = load_p(filepath)
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error loading {filepath}: {e}")
        sys.exit(1)

    data = validate()
    ok = test_all(p, data)
    sys.exit(0 if ok else 1)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

# Task 004 Insights

## Transformation (from gen.py)
Input = parallelogram outlines. Output = shift the non-bottom-row parts right by 1
(bottom edge unchanged; rightmost column / "cap" unchanged). Shapes are in vertical
row-bands separated by blank rows; one color per shape.

## KEY minimal rule (found by brute-force search over arc-gen)
Pad each row with a leading 0. For output cell at row r (r < last), col c:
  out[c] = a[c-1] if b[c-1] < any(b) else a[c]
where a=row r, b=row r+1 (the row below), any(b)=whether row below has any nonzero.
The LAST grid row always copies (input==output) -> just append g[-1:].

Why it works: within a same-color run, "keep a[c]" and "shift-in a[c-1]" give the
same value; the b[c-1]<any(b) test only matters at boundaries. Search proved this
is essentially the UNIQUE minimal 1-comparison rule (needs a[c-1], a[c], b[c-1], any(b)).

Verified: 0 failures on all 262 arc-gen, 3/3 validate, AND 3000 random gen.generate().

## BEST: workdir/v22.py = 91 bytes (no trailing newline)
```python
p=lambda g:[[[y,x][u<any(b)]for x,y,u in zip([0]+a,a,[0]+b)]for a,b in zip(g,g[1:])]+g[-1:]
```
- zip([0]+a,a,[0]+b) -> (x,y,u)=(a[c-1],a[c],b[c-1])
- [y,x][u<any(b)] : index-select (shorter than ternary)
- +g[-1:] : appends last row (copies), avoids sentinel row

## Progression
769->393->219->190->178->172->163->158->153->117->106->101->93->92->91

## Notes
- verify.py only tests gen.validate() (3 examples) but I keep full generality.
- No 3rd-party libs; no web.
- Trailing newline counts as a byte -> write files WITHOUT it (use printf).
- Ideas that did NOT help: pre-padding rows via walrus (outer zip cost > inline [0]+ save),
  arithmetic instead of index-select, using b[c] instead of b[c-1] (fails at top-left corner).

content.thinking:
I'm working through the transformation rule for each cell: the output at position [r][c] takes the value from [r][c-1] if the cell below-left is zero and there's at least one non-zero value in the row below, otherwise it keeps the original value. The condition `b[c-1] < any(b)` simplifies to checking if `b[c-1] == 0` since `any(b)` is either 0 or 1.

For the wrapping approach using `g[1:]+g`, the last row gets g[0] (all zeros) as its "below" row, so `any(g[0])` is 0, which means no shift happens and the last row just copies itself—that's the correct behavior.

Now I'm trying to find a fundamentally different approach that's shorter than 86 bytes. So the bottom row stays unchanged, the top row shifts right by 1, and the diagonal rows also shift right by 1 but with a cap at the right edge to prevent going past the grid boundary. The key constraint is that the right diagonal input can't exceed the grid width, so there's a min() operation that clamps the output position. On the last diagonal row, the right point caps at col+wide-1, so it doesn't actually shift—this is the "cap cell stays" case that the per-cell rule already handles. Now I'm moving to a mask-based approach for the whole grid, where I'll track the current row and the row below it, then shift the current row right by 1 and selectively keep cells based on a mask condition.

A cell shouldn't shift when either the cell below-left is nonzero or the entire row below is all zeros. Equivalently, shifting happens only when the cell below-left is zero and the row below has at least some nonzero content. For non-bottom rows within a shape, the row below always has nonzero cells, so I shift cell c right precisely when the cell directly below-left is zero—that's the key insight here.

Now I'm reconsidering whether there's a simpler way to express this. The output is essentially the input shifted right by one position, except bottom rows and right boundaries stay in place. I could frame this as: each output cell either keeps its input value or takes the value from the left, depending on a mask. Within a continuous run of nonzero cells, the values are identical anyway, so the choice only matters at boundaries—specifically at the left edge of a run where the cell to the left is zero.

Let me try a brute-force search to find a shorter expression. First I need to verify what the current best solution actually is.

6	```

10	1. Reduce 5 zip streams -> 3 streams (a, a[1:]+[0], b) giving (y,z,v). The left-neighbor
11	   shifted-in term F[c-1] is carried via a walrus `s` instead of streaming x=a[c-1],u=b[c-1].
12	   F[c] = any(b)*a[c]*(b[c]<=a[c+1]).  output[c] = a[c] + F[c-1] - F[c].
13	   Walrus: `y+s-(s:=any(b)*y*(v<=z))` reads old s (=F[c-1]) then assigns new s (=F[c]).
14	2. Fold any(b) INTO the walrus assignment (s already carries the any(b) factor), so the outer
15	   `any(b)*(...)` parens disappear. Saved 2.
16	3. Per-row reset of s to 0 via `if[s:=0]` (a truthy list literal [0] that also assigns s=0).
17	   8 bytes; cheaper than `if(s:=0)==0` (11).
18	4. Row pairing `zip(g,g[1:]+g)` — zip truncates to len(g); last row pairs with g[0] which is
19	   ALWAYS all-zero (verified). Shorter than g[1:]+g[:1]. (Credit: teammate.)
20	
21	## Dead ends
22	- Carrying s across rows WITHOUT reset (param s=0): fails 46. F[last] of a row is not always 0
23	  (last column not always blank), so stale s leaks into next row's column 0. Reset is required.
24	- Folding reset into the z-pad `a[1:]+[s:=0]`: SyntaxError "assignment expression cannot be used
25	  in a comprehension iterable expression". Walrus is banned in the comprehension iterable.
26	- `a[1:]+a` truncation for the z-pad: needs left column all-zero; it isn't. Also longer wouldn't help.
27	- Two-pass (define F list then zip(a,F,[0]+F)): the `for F in[[...]]` binding overhead makes it ~130+.
28	- max(b)/sum(b) instead of any(b): they scale the difference by the color value; wrong.
29	
30	## Data properties (arc-gen, 262 items)
31	- g[0] (first row) always all-zero. Last row NOT always zero. Left/last columns NOT always zero.

## Key ideas that got from 119 -> 104

3. Per-row reset of s to 0 via `if[s:=0]` (a truthy list literal [0] that also assigns s=0).

"""Aggressive template search for sub-86. Tests on quick sample first, full-verify winners."""
import sys, json, random
sys.path.insert(0, '.')
from gen import validate
import gen

D = validate()
VAL = [(ex['input'], ex['output']) for s in ('train', 'test') for ex in D[s]]
ARC = [(e['input'], e['output']) for e in json.load(open('data.json'))['arc-gen']]
random.seed(3)
RND = [(lambda ex: (ex['input'], ex['output']))(gen.generate()) for _ in range(2000)]
QUICK = VAL + ARC + RND[:200]
FULL = VAL + ARC + RND


def test(code, cases):
    try:
        G = {}
        exec(code, G)
        p = G['p']
    except Exception as e:
        return False
    for inp, out in cases:
        try:
            if p(inp) != out:
                return False
        except Exception:
            return False
    return True


# Programmatic candidate generation
sels = [
    "[y,x][u<any(b)]",
    "[y,x][u<max(b)]",
    "(y,x)[u<any(b)]",
]
# form A: streams ([0]+a, a, [0]+b), no prepend
formA = "p=lambda g:[[%s for x,y,u in zip([0]+a,a,[0]+b)]for a,b in zip(g,g[1:]+g)]"
# form B: prepend 0, streams (a, a[1:], b)
formB = "p=lambda g:[[0]+[%s for x,y,u in zip(a,a[1:],b)]for a,b in zip(g,g[1:]+g)]"

cands = set()
for s in sels:
    cands.add(formA % s)
    cands.add(formB % s)

# extra hand variants
cands.update([
    # walrus to hoist any(b) once per row inside inner (won't help but measure)
    "p=lambda g:[[0]+[[y,x][u<A]for x,y,u in zip(a,a[1:],b)]for a,b in zip(g,g[1:]+g)if(A:=any(b))or 1]",
    # combine using arithmetic
    "p=lambda g:[[0]+[y+(x-y)*(u<any(b))for x,y,u in zip(a,a[1:],b)]for a,b in zip(g,g[1:]+g)]",
    # star pairing via zip of tuples
    "p=lambda g:[[0]+[[y,x][u<any(b)]for(x,u),(y,_)in zip(t:=[*zip(a,b)],t[1:])]for a,b in zip(g,g[1:]+g)]",
    # use *g for below? no
])

results = []
for c in sorted(cands, key=len):
    if test(c, QUICK):
        if test(c, FULL):
            results.append((len(c), c, 'FULL-OK'))
        else:
            results.append((len(c), c, 'quick-only'))
    else:
        results.append((len(c), c, 'FAIL'))

for n, c, tag in sorted(results):
    print(n, tag, c)

ok = [(n, c) for n, c, t in results if t == 'FULL-OK']
sub = [x for x in ok if x[0] < 86]
print('\nBest FULL-OK:', min(ok) if ok else None)
print('Sub-86 FULL-OK:', sub if sub else 'NONE')

## Result: 82 bytes (beats the 86-byte target by 4 bytes)

**Shortest verified-OK solution:**
```
p=lambda g:[[a[c-(b[c-1]<any(b))]for c in range(len(a))]for a,b in zip(g,g[1:]+g)]
```
- 82 bytes, verified via both my checker (`bytes 82 OK 82` = 3 validate + 262 arc-gen + 2000 random seed-3) and the official `python3 verify.py` (3/3 PASS).
- Saved at `/Users/jackson/Desktop/code-golf/deepseek-v4-pro-default/task004/workdir/agentC_alt_best82.py`.

**Why it's a fundamentally different structure than the 86-byte double-zip:** instead of building three zip streams `zip([0]+a,a,[0]+b)` and index-selecting `[y,x][u<any(b)]`, this uses **index arithmetic**: `a[c-(b[c-1]<any(b))]` subtracts a 0/1 shift directly from the column index (shift → `a[c-1]`, no-shift → `a[c]`), collapsing the select into a single `a[...]`. The `b[c-1]` at column 0 wraps to `b[-1]`, which is load-bearing (a different offset like `[0]+b[:-1]` breaks output col 0). `g[1:]+g` uses zip-truncation to pair the last grid row with the always-blank `g[0]`.

**Could not beat 82.** I explored many different structures myself (single-prepend shifted-window select, whole-grid mask, arithmetic pixel math `y+(x-y)*m`, walrus-carry, map/enumerate forms) and spawned three parallel search agents (golf-tricks, whole-grid, grammar brute-force). Every valid rearrangement lands at 82 (a family of ~16 equivalent forms, e.g. `~-c` for `c-1`, `max(b)` for `any(b)` — all exactly 82) or longer. The wall is `range(len(a))` (needed because the value expression packs two indices `a[c]`/`a[c-1]` into one `a[c-shift]`); dropping it via enumerate (+1), zip-with-range (+11), or rotate-stream select forms (+4…+12) is strictly longer. The `any(b)` gate is genuinely required — `sum(b)` fails on multi-colored-cell rows; removing it shifts the bottom edge wrongly.
